query: incidence rate of primary spinal intradural MPNST in neurofibromatosis patients

retrieved_docs.title: Conus and Cauda Equina Tumors -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 55% of spinal lesions are extradural and involve the vertebral column. Of these, 20% are located in the lumbar spine. The most common lesion is metastasis. Approximately 40% of spinal lesions are intradural extramedullary. These are generally meningiomas of the dural sac, as well as schwannomas and neurofibromas of the nerve roots. Approximately 5% of spinal lesions are intramedullary (greater than 90% are ependymoma or astrocytoma of varying histological grades, and less than 2% are metastases).

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis Type 2 -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple spinal tumors can also be seen in patients with NF2, including schwannomas, meningiomas, and ependymomas, and can be diagnosed with MRI. Schwannomas are the most common type; they typically arise from the dorsal root with the typical appearance of a dumbbell shape with the widening of the neural foramen and intense enhancement. Meningiomas enhance extramedullary lesions, typically seen in the cervical or thoracic region. Ependymomas are intramedullary lesions causing enlargement of the spinal cord with hemorrhage, cystic changes, and variable enhancement. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Lisch Nodules -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with NF1 are at high risk of developing both benign and malignant tumors. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNSTs) occur in about 2% to 5% of NF1 patients. [13] They are high-grade sarcomas and originate from tissues of mesenchymal origin. A rapid change in the size of a preexisting neurofibroma, unremitting pain, intralesional hemorrhage, and infiltration of the adjacent structures indicate a possible malignant transformation to MPNST. The malignant transformation of a neurofibroma has an extremely poor prognosis with recurrences and distant metastasis. Studies have shown the average 5-year survival rate for these patients ranges from 16% to 52%. [31] Hence, patients with neurofibromatosis should be closely monitored for possible malignancy as this sarcomatous change in a neurofibroma has an extremely poor prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Intramedullary Spinal Cord Tumors -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although intramedullary spinal cord tumors are mostly sporadic, some are associated with clinical syndromes, such as neurofibromatosis 1 or 2 (NF-1, NF-2) and Von Hippel-Lindau disease. NF-1 is due to a mutation on chromosome 17, which encodes the tumor suppressor gene neurofibromin. About 19% of patients with NF-1 develop an intramedullary spinal cord tumor. NF-1 is largely associated with neurofibromas and intradural extramedullary-type spinal cord tumors. NF-2, caused by a mutation of the tumor suppressor protein schwannomin/merlin on chromosome 22, is reported in 2% of patients with intramedullary spinal cord tumors. Patients with NF-2 more commonly present with ependymomas and occasionally meningiomas (extramedullary). In Von Hippel-Lindau disease, hemangioblastomas are the most frequent intramedullary spinal cord tumors. Overall, astrocytomas are the most commonly encountered intramedullary spinal cord tumors. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis Type 2 -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of NF2 is about 1 in 25,000 to 40,000 individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurocutaneous Syndromes -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with NF1, there is also an increase in the incidence of pheochromocytoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, leukemia, and Wilms tumor, compared with the general population. [10] Due to increased risk for scoliosis, patients should have a yearly screening. In NF2, the need for treatment is only if the tumors become symptomatic, and the treatment of choice would be surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningioma -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Spinal meningiomas account for 30.7 to 38.8% of all primary intradural spinal tumors in the United States in those patients aged 20 years and older, with an age-adjusted incidence of 0.193 per 100,000 of the population. [1] These meningiomas occur at a frequency of 80.3% in females. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis Type 1 -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Discuss the pathophysiology of neurofibromatosis-1. Describe the appropriate examination and workup of neurofibromatosis-1. Review the management of patients with neurofibromatosis-1. Summarize how interprofessional coordinated activity and information sharing can improve patient care and outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis Type 1 -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tumors are treated based on the type, involvement of other structures, and associated complications. Radiation therapy in NF-1 is associated with the emergence of secondary tumors, thereby opting for radiation only in essential cases. [53] Cutaneous and nodular neurofibromas are treated when they develop symptoms like pain, increase in size, weakness, vision loss, or cosmetic concerns. Surgery, laser, and electrodesiccation are some of the treatment modalities. Gabapentin has sometimes been utilized for pruritus treatment along with neuropathic pain management. In particular, plexiform neurofibromas (PN) are associated with high morbidity and malignant transformation compared to cutaneous and nodular neurofibromas. Compression of the airway or spinal cord and disfigurement are common reasons for the surgical removal of plexiform neurofibromas. [54] Selumetinib was approved for use in children three years of age and older with PN that cannot be operated on despite having symptoms or being noted to be making progress. Selumetinib is the first drug approved by US food and drug administration for plexiform NF. [43] It is a mitogen-activated protein kinase inhibitor. [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: NF1 is characterized by the presence of neurofibromas, cafe-au-lait spots, freckling, optic gliomas, and bony dysplasias. Bilateral vestibular schwannomas and meningiomas are characteristic of NF2; however, neurofibromas do not occur in this syndrome. Therefore, the name NF2 is a misnomer, and a new and more accurate name for NF2-related schwannomatosis has been established based on an international consensus recommendation. Neurofibromatosis types 1 and 2 treatment involves clinical monitoring and medical intervention when appropriate. Schwannomatosis is the rarest form, marked by multiple peripheral and spinal schwannomas, often causing chronic pain. Vestibular involvement is uncommon, differentiating it from NF2.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis -- Introduction -- Schwanomatosis
retrieved_docs.content: Schwanomatosis is the rarest of the 3 types of neurofibromatosis, with an incidence of 0.58 cases per 1,000,000 people. The presence of multiple nonintradermal peripheral and spinal schwanomas characterizes it. Localized or diffuse chronic pain or asymptomatic masses are common presentations. Vestibular schwannoma is uncommon in this entity. [7] Schwannomatosis is mostly sporadic. Approximately 15% to 25% of these patients have inherited this disorder. SMARCB1 and LZTR1 are the 2 most common gene mutations, either spontaneous or inherited with reduced penetrance. The newly revised names for these entities are SMARCB1 -related schwannomatosis and LZTR1 -related schwannomatosis. [3] Due to its rarity, schwanomatosis will not be elaborated on in this activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis Type 1 -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Serial evaluation with WBMRI should be assessed based on the clinical and NF1 domains. [15] Disease and radiological progression are higher, with NF1 mutation affecting GRD domains. [15] The primary contributing variable to mortality among patients with NF1 includes brain tumors and MPNSTs. [4] Both recent and ongoing research trials have primarily been focused on PN only (approximately 45%). [72]

retrieved_docs.title: Intramedullary Spinal Cord Tumors -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology and underlying factors contributing to the development of intramedullary spinal cord tumors. Determine the clinical presentation of a patient with an intramedullary spinal cord tumor, detailing the specific symptoms and signs they may exhibit and the correlation between specific sensory and motor deficits and the affected regions of the spinal cord. Develop a systematic approach for evaluating intramedullary spinal cord tumors, and determine the treatment and management options available for these tumors. Collaborate with interdisciplinary healthcare team members to optimize patient care and outcomes in the management of intramedullary spinal cord tumors. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis -- Prognosis -- Neurofibromatosis Type 2
retrieved_docs.content: Schwannomas and meningiomas are benign tumors and are generally amenable to neurosurgical therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis Type 2 -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Small asymptomatic vestibular schwannomas can be managed conservatively with MRI follow-up. Surgery is the primary treatment for large symptomatic vestibular schwannomas. Meningiomas are also treated surgically, with radiation treatment reserved for non-surgical candidates.[8] Spinal cord ependymomas are usually low-grade tumors and can be followed clinically, with surgery reserved for symptomatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurofibromatosis Type 1 -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Neurofibromatosis-1 (NF-1), or Von Recklinghausen disease, is one of the inheritable neurocutaneous disorders manifested by developmental changes in the nervous system, bones, and skin. It is an autosomal dominant disorder. Additionally, it is the most common amongst all the hamartoma neoplastic syndromes, including tuberous sclerosis, Gardner, and Cowden syndromes. Other types of neurofibromatosis are Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) and schwannomatosis. Cafe-au-lait macules and neurofibromas are the distinguishing features of NF1. NF2 can have similar cutaneous manifestations as that of NF1. But the hallmark manifestations of NF2 include schwannoma, meningioma, and ependymoma. This activity describes the clinical presentation and management of NF-1 and highlights the interprofessional team's role in managing patients with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Laminectomy; excision intervertebral disc procedures are associated with the development of post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية). Post‑laminectomy syndrome is classified as a subtype of spine disorder (اضطراب العمود الفقري). This relationship highlights that patients undergoing laminectomy or disc excision may experience persistent pain and functional impairment characteristic of this spinal disorder, underscoring the need for careful postoperative management and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1713
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology within various anatomical regions. Specific operations that are classified as methods of exploration include partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, whether accompanied by cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, is regarded as an exploration method. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Orthopedic exploration of the spinal canal is achieved through laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; this technique is applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and sacral regions, and may be listed as a possibly included method in broader procedural codes. Additional exploration procedures encompass nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, which can be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, including specific transthoracic mediastinal exploration. Collectively, these diverse surgical methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ system and clinical objective, serving both diagnostic and therapeutic roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1421
retrieved_docs.content: The lumbar puncture (البزل القطني, LP) is clinically associated with headache (صداع), a common post‑procedural complication that patients may experience after the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 97

Spinal exploration includes cervical, thoracic, lumbar, and sacral laminectomy/laminotomy techniques that provide exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; single‑segment procedures involve one to two vertebral levels, whereas multi‑segment procedures involve more than two levels. Excision is the operative method underlying many of these exploration procedures and serves as the parent concept for “Clinical actions A‑G,” which in turn parents the broader Exploration procedure class. Excision is applied to partial or complete adrenalectomy; cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including all cholangiographic and sphincterotomy variants);

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and the hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all clinical entities that have the nerve as their finding site, indicating that pathological changes in these conditions are localized to nerve tissue. Likewise, an incision of nerve is a direct procedural intervention performed on the nerve, underscoring the nerve as a common target for both disease processes and surgical treatment. Recognizing that the nerve serves as the anatomical locus for these diverse findings and procedures helps clinicians accurately diagnose neuropathic conditions, plan appropriate interventions, and coordinate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1003
retrieved_docs.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) is broadly related to the field of neurosurgery (جراحة الأعصاب), as neurosurgery encompasses the surgical diagnosis, treatment, and management of a wide variety of disorders affecting both the central and peripheral components of the nervous system. This overarching relationship highlights that many neurological conditions—ranging from traumatic injuries and vascular malformations to tumors and degenerative diseases—require neurosurgical expertise. Recognizing the extensive connection between the nervous system and neurosurgical practice is essential for comprehensive patient assessment, interdisciplinary collaboration, and the delivery of effective, targeted therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly inspect, access, or remove pathology from specific anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland surgery such as adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by several cholecystectomy variants that incorporate common duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, each designed to evaluate and treat ductal stones or strictures. Cranial exploration is covered by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, which allow direct visualization of intracranial lesions. Orbital pathology can be addressed through a transcranial approach to explore the orbit and excise lesions. Spinal exploration procedures include laminectomies—cervical, sacral, lumbar, thoracic, or multilevel—performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina in conditions such as spinal stenosis; these may be listed as core methods or as possibly included techniques depending on the extent of vertebral involvement. A related laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) technique provides nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated disc material at a single lumbar level, often as a re‑exploration. Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach exemplifies neck and mediastinal access for hyperparathyroidism. All of these specific methods fall under the broader “Exploration procedure” classification, which itself is organized under the parent category “Clinical actions A‑G.” This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse surgical explorations are grouped for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.
